Q1: I tried to keep things pretty consistent, honestly. I'd usually study in the evenings after dinner, around 7 or 8 PM, for a few hours. I'm the kind of person who needs to physically do things to learn, so I'd often write out notes by hand or work through problems on paper. My routine was there, but I can't say I was always fully into it—sometimes I was just going through the motions because that's what I was supposed to do.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, like every weekday evening and some weekend mornings. But yeah, when deadlines came up, everything would shift and I'd cram more hours in. It wasn't ideal, but the routine gave me something to fall back on even when things got stressful. I just did what needed to be done to keep up.
Q3: Usually it was because I had to check if there was a new assignment posted or if a deadline was coming up. Sometimes I'd get a notification or remember that I hadn't logged in for a couple days. It wasn't really excitement that brought me there—more like obligation, you know? I'd log in, do what I needed, and log out.
Q4: After a few weeks, logging in became kind of automatic, like brushing my teeth. I'd just do it without thinking too much about it. It happened because the course had regular weekly tasks, so I knew I had to check in at least a couple times a week. It wasn't something I looked forward to, but it became part of my weekly rhythm.
Q5: The video lectures were actually helpful because I could pause and rewind when I needed to work through something hands-on. I'd often watch a bit, then try to replicate what was shown on my own. The discussion forums were okay too when other students explained things in simpler terms. Those felt more useful than just reading dense text.
Q6: I definitely skipped over a lot of the optional readings and supplementary materials. They felt like extras that wouldn't directly help my grades, so I couldn't justify spending time on them. When you're already putting in a lot of hours and feeling kind of drained, you have to pick your battles. I focused on what was required and let the rest slide.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was clearly going to help me on an assignment or exam. If I could see the direct connection between doing it and getting a better grade, I'd put in the effort. Anything that felt vague or disconnected from assessments just didn't make the cut for me. I needed that practical payoff.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—like they were nice-to-haves but not essential. Required ones had this weight to them because I knew they'd affect my grade. I'd always do the required stuff first, and honestly, by the time I was done with those, I didn't have much energy left for optional things. They just fell off my radar.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then print them out so I could write notes in the margins. I also used my textbook quite a bit, flipping through chapters and highlighting things. Being a hands-on learner, I retained more when I was physically interacting with materials rather than just staring at a screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd be at my desk working through practice problems on paper for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. Or I'd be reviewing printed notes before bed. That kind of studying doesn't leave a digital trace at all. The system only sees when I'm clicking around online, not when I'm actually absorbing information.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, take notes, and then never go back to it online. Instead, I'd review my handwritten notes or the screenshots I took. It was more efficient for me to study from my own materials than to keep logging back in. The initial access was just to get what I needed, then I'd work with it offline.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially around midterms when everything piled up. I was feeling pretty sad and stressed during those times, and it was hard to muster the energy to engage. Some days I'd just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Having peer support helped a bit, but the heaviness was still there underneath.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or had other responsibilities piling up, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS. I'd skim through materials instead of really engaging with them. Fatigue was a big one—after studying for hours, my brain would just shut off. I'd log in but I wasn't really present, if that makes sense.
Q14: Yeah, more times than I'd like to admit. I'd open a video and let it play while my mind wandered somewhere else. Or I'd scroll through readings without actually absorbing the words. It was like my body was doing the motions but my brain had checked out. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though technically I was 'active.'
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they felt draining. I knew they were important for participation marks, but putting my thoughts out there for others to see felt like a lot of effort when I was already feeling low. I'd tell myself I'd do it later, and sometimes later never came. It wasn't that I didn't care—I just couldn't bring myself to do it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator. Without them, I probably would have fallen even further behind. I'd check the LMS more frequently as deadlines approached and cram in work at the last minute. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was what got me to actually complete things. The routine helped, but deadlines were the real driver.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got good feedback, it gave me a small boost and made me want to try harder on the next thing. But when feedback was critical or vague, it kind of deflated me. Given how I was already feeling, negative feedback could send me into a slump where I'd avoid the course for a day or two.
Q18: The weekly structure helped me stay on track because I knew what to expect. Having regular deadlines meant I couldn't completely fall off. But the structure also felt rigid sometimes—like I was just ticking boxes rather than actually learning. It kept me compliant but didn't really inspire me to go beyond the minimum.
Q19: The clear deadlines and organized modules made it easier to know what was expected. But some of the content felt very text-heavy, which isn't great for someone like me who learns by doing. More interactive elements or hands-on activities would have helped me engage better. The design was functional but not particularly motivating.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to understand what's expected without actually doing the work. Or they could be like me sometimes—logged in and clicking around but not mentally present. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity. You can spend hours on a platform and still not accomplish much.
Q21: That actually sounds a bit like me. I didn't have super high LMS activity, but I studied a lot offline and still managed a decent grade. The platform doesn't capture everything. If you're efficient and know how to study independently, you don't need to be logged in constantly to do well. Quality matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on. But they miss the depth of engagement—whether you're actually thinking or just scrolling. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or how much you're really understanding. It's like measuring the surface without seeing what's underneath.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real mental effort into it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being present and trying to understand. For me, true engagement feels rare because I often go through the motions without that deeper connection. When I am engaged, I feel it in my focus and curiosity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally this semester or how the peer support sessions actually helped me keep going. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes at my desk or the times I helped a classmate understand something over text. The human side of learning—the feelings, the relationships, the offline effort—that's all invisible to the data.
